text: Hello, I'm sharing a elective outpatient cholecystectomy in a patient who had minor pain and an ultrasound confirming cholecystitis. They had done a course of antibiotics just for formality by their gastroenterologist and noted no change in symptoms because they were actually otherwise completely asymptomatic by the time they got the report of the ultrasound. The only symptom really was some vague epigastric discomfort that they actually thought was chest pain. So of course, surprised to see how bad the gallbladder was. And the patient on the morning of surgery said that they were so asymptomatic, they didn't even know if they needed surgery.

The surgeon attempts dissection starting at the bottom of the gallbladder, which is tense and difficult to grasp. The liver is pushed upwards to expose the common bile duct, which is visible, while the cystic duct is not illuminated. Thickened tissue is observed to the right of the presumed area of concern. The surgeon dissects around the gallbladder to separate it from the liver, eventually creating a hole in the gallbladder to decompress it. The bile does not light up due to obstruction in the cystic duct, preventing ICG from entering.

The surgeon attempts a top-down dissection approach, then switches to dissecting from below, identifying the common bile duct and artery over the gallbladder. The gallbladder appears fused to surrounding tissue, complicating efforts to access the cystic duct. The surgeon clears presumed safe tissue to identify the duct, revealing a hole in the gallbladder, which is partially intrahepatic.

The surgeon approaches the gallbladder from behind, performing a top-down dissection. The tissue appears thick and indistinct, prompting concerns about potential injury. The gallbladder is opened to facilitate identification of the cystic duct. Stones within the gallbladder are crushed using a grasper and suctioned out, effectively clearing them without spilling into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon cuts further down the gallbladder, removing additional stones from the presumed location of the cystic duct. Despite expectations, no bile or green ICG appears. The surgeon continues dissection from a top-down approach, reaching the level of the artery, and carefully clears tissue posteriorly to lift the bottom of the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies more stones deep within the gallbladder and decides to perform a subtotal cholecystectomy, cutting the majority of the gallbladder down to the bottom. The artery is cleared and divided to improve visibility of the cystic duct. The surgeon inspects the cystic duct from inside the gallbladder, ensuring no stones or bile are present, and proceeds to sew the remaining gallbladder closed, confirming the stitches do not encroach upon the common duct.

I was happy with my closure. Take a look again. There's the common duct. Clean things up. I did leave a drain. I did get post-op labs that evening and the next morning, which were normal. And the drain was removed and the patient was sent home. I hope you enjoyed.
